sessions.title: Ideation
sessions.set_the_stage:
The room opens with one question on the screen. What would you build if you had the tools and the time? Five minutes of silence. Everyone writes something down.

Then two things only.

The solopreneur frame. You are not a student today. You are a solopreneur. You have a problem, real tools, and a deadline. Everything that happens from this moment lives inside that frame.

The HMV lens. Three questions demonstrated live on one idea from the room. Fast. Visceral. Shows what the lens does to a half-formed idea before a single word is explained.

questions.title: What is the idea?
questions.body: Every participant reads what they wrote on their card. That is what you want to build. What problem does it solve? Describe the problem without mentioning your solution.
questions.facilitator_note: Listen for one thing — is this an idea or a solution? Most people write solutions. Push them back to the problem.

questions.title: Is this a real problem?
questions.body: Have you personally experienced this problem? Have you watched someone else struggle with it? Or does it just seem like something that should exist?
questions.facilitator_note: Three levels of problem reality. Level 1 — personally felt — strongest. Level 2 — watched someone struggle — strong. Level 3 — think it probably exists — weakest, needs validation before building.

questions.title: Who specifically has this problem?
questions.body: Not everyone. Not professionals. Not students. One specific type of person in one specific situation.
questions.facilitator_note: The test is whether the participant could identify this person at a party. If yes, specific enough. If no, keep going.

questions.title: How often does this problem occur?
questions.body: Does this person experience this problem daily, weekly, monthly, or occasionally?
questions.facilitator_note: Frequency alone does not determine value. Frequency multiplied by cost does.

questions.title: What does it cost them?
questions.body: Not just time or money. What does it cost them emotionally? What do they feel when this problem appears? What do they miss out on because it exists?
questions.facilitator_note: The emotional cost is what converts a curious person into a paying customer.

questions.title: What have they already tried?
questions.body: If this problem is real, people have tried to solve it. What tools, workarounds, or habits do they currently use? Why is that not good enough?
questions.facilitator_note: This question reveals the competitive landscape and the gap at the same time.

questions.title: Will they pay for a solution?
questions.body: If a solution existed that worked exactly as promised, what would it be worth to this person?
questions.facilitator_note: Willingness to pay is not about price. It is about whether the problem is acute enough.

techniques: The question is not what should I build. The question is who am I building for and what do they actually feel?
Every idea must start with a real human the participant can describe in enough detail that the room could recognise them.

Ask participants to close their eyes and picture the specific person who has this problem. Where are they? What time of day is it? What just happened that triggered the problem? What are they feeling?
The question is not can AI solve this. The question is should AI solve this.
Failure mode one — the AI-for-the-sake-of-AI idea. The participant wants to use AI and is finding a problem to justify it.

Failure mode two — the spreadsheet problem. The problem could be solved adequately with a well-designed form or spreadsheet. Failure mode three — the human judgment problem. Every step requires genuine human empathy or ethical reasoning.
The question is not is this interesting. The question is is there a real value exchange?
Signal one — someone currently pays money to have this problem managed. Signal two — someone currently spends significant time on this problem.
Signal three — someone currently experiences a painful consequence when this problem is not solved.

prompts.title: Claude — Stress Test Through HMV
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur exploring an idea for a new venture. I need you to stress test it honestly.

My idea: [one sentence]
My target user: [specific person in specific situation]
The problem they experience: [specific problem, not the solution]
How often they experience it: [frequency]
What it costs them: [time, money, emotional cost]
What they currently do about it: [existing workaround]
Why the workaround is not good enough: [the gap]

Run this through three lenses and be direct.

Humanics — is my target user specific enough? Is the problem I described genuinely felt by a real human or am I building something for a demographic I imagine?

Mechanics — is AI genuinely useful here? Should AI solve this or am I forcing it? Name the failure mode if you see one.

Valuenomics — is there a real value exchange? Would this specific person pay for a solution?

End with three hard questions I should be able to answer before I start building.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Both prompts run simultaneously. Give participants two minutes to fill in every blank from the discussion before pasting.

prompts.title: Perplexity — Validate the Problem
prompts.content:
Find evidence that the following problem is real.

Problem: [their problem statement]
Target user: [specific person and situation]

Search for Reddit discussions, LinkedIn posts, forum threads, news articles, and research where real people describe experiencing this exact frustration. Give me five specific examples with quotes where possible. Tell me how widespread this problem appears to be and whether any existing solutions are addressing it well.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Participants read both responses and adjust their problem statement where Claude or Perplexity reveals a weakness.

build.title: Both prompts run simultaneously (10–15 minutes)
build.instructions: Claude asks three hard questions at the end of its response. The participant must answer them. Some answers reveal a weakness in the idea. The participant strengthens the idea or pivots to a better one.

build.title: Read the evidence
build.instructions: Perplexity returns real evidence. Real people describing real pain in their own words. The participant reads it and feels confirmation or correction.

test_output.title: The Specificity Test
test_output.description: Can you describe your target user in enough detail that someone in this room could identify them on the street? If no, return to Question 3.

test_output.title: The Reality Test
test_output.description: Can you name one specific real person, not a friend, who has this problem right now? If no, the problem may not be real enough.

test_output.title: The Pain Test
test_output.description: Can you describe what your target user feels emotionally when this problem occurs? If no, return to Question 5.

test_output.title: The Gap Test
test_output.description: Can you explain specifically why existing solutions are not good enough? If no, the opportunity may not be real.

test_output.title: The Value Test
test_output.description: Can you name one specific reason this person would pay for a solution? If no, return to Question 7.

sessions.title: Design
sessions.set_the_stage:
You now have a real problem with a real human at its centre.

Most people at this point make one mistake. They open a tool and start building immediately. They figure out what they are building as they go. That is how you waste four hours and end up with something that does not work.

A solopreneur who has built before does something different. They spend thirty minutes designing before they touch a single tool. Not a detailed specification. Not a project plan. Just enough clarity to know exactly what they are building before they start.

Three things. What is the simplest possible version of the solution? What does the user put in and what do they get out? What does the AI do and what does it never do?

Answer those three questions in the next thirty minutes and you will build twice as fast and twice as well.

questions.title: What is the simplest possible version?
questions.body: Describe your solution in one sentence. Not the full vision. Not the ideal version. The minimum version that still creates real value for your target user.
questions.facilitator_note: Push until it is one thing that does one job well.

questions.title: What does the user put in?
questions.body: What is the minimum information your user needs to provide for the solution to work?
questions.facilitator_note: Every field you add is friction. Friction reduces completion. What is the absolute minimum?

questions.title: What does the user get out?
questions.body: What exactly appears when the solution runs? The output format must serve the user not the builder.
questions.facilitator_note: A busy executive needs something they can read in two minutes. A student needs something that explains its reasoning.

questions.title: What does the AI do at each step?
questions.body: Walk through what the AI is actually doing. Step one, it receives this. Step two, it does this. Step three, it produces this.
questions.facilitator_note: Listen for magical thinking and push back toward reality.

questions.title: Where does the human step in?
questions.body: At what point does a human need to review the AI output before it reaches the user?
questions.facilitator_note: Every AI product has a human handoff point. Design it deliberately, not accidentally.

questions.title: What should the AI never do?
questions.body: Name three things your AI must never do regardless of what the user asks.
questions.facilitator_note: Constraints are not limitations. They are trust builders.

techniques: The confusion test — would your target user immediately understand what to do?
The delight test — at what moment does the user feel genuine relief or satisfaction?
The trust test — at what moment could the user feel uncertain? Design to address it.
The reliability test — can the AI produce consistently good output for the full range of inputs?
The failure test — what happens when the AI gets it wrong? Is it recoverable?
The constraint test — are the constraints specific enough to actually constrain behaviour?
The clarity test — does the user immediately understand what they received and why it is valuable?
The action test — does the output tell the user what to do next?
The worth test — would a user look at this output and feel it was worth the effort to get it?

prompts.title: Define the Minimum Solution
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur designing my first AI-powered product. I need to define the simplest possible version that still creates real value.

My problem statement: [from Session 1]
My target user: [from Session 1]
My value proposition: [from Session 1]

Help me define the minimum viable version of this solution. Then give me the solution in one sentence and the three-step user flow.

Step 1 — the user inputs [what].
Step 2 — the AI does [what].
Step 3 — the user receives [what in what format].

Be specific. Be simple. Push back if I am making this more complex than it needs to be.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: First of three prompts. Participant confirms the output is simple and specific. Makes one or two edits before moving on.

prompts.title: Write the System Prompt
prompts.content:
I am building the following AI-powered product.

Solution in one sentence: [from Prompt 1]
Target user: [from Session 1]
What the user inputs: [from Prompt 1]
What the AI produces: [from Prompt 1]
Three things the AI must never do: [from Question 6]

Write me a system prompt with seven elements.

Role — who the AI is and what it exists to do.
Context — what the AI needs to know about the situation.
Instructions — step by step what the AI does when triggered.
Tone — how the AI communicates.
Output format — exactly how the AI structures its output.
Constraints — the three things the AI never does.
Escalation — when the AI should stop and tell the user to seek human help.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Participant reads every element. Makes two or three specific edits.

prompts.title: Test the System Prompt
prompts.content:
Read the system prompt you just wrote. Now play the role of my target user.

My target user is: [human portrait]

Give me three inputs this user might realistically provide. For each input, run the system prompt mentally and tell me what output it would produce. Is that output genuinely useful?

Then give me one edge case input. What happens? Does the system prompt handle it gracefully or does it break?
prompts.facilitator_instruction: If the edge case breaks the prompt, the participant asks Claude to fix it before moving on.

build.title: Three prompts run in sequence (~15 minutes total)
build.instructions: Prompt 1 produces the minimum solution and three-step user flow. Participant confirms it is simple and specific. Makes one or two edits.

build.title: System prompt review
build.instructions: Prompt 2 produces the system prompt. Participant reads every element. Makes two or three specific edits.

build.title: Edge case test
build.instructions: Prompt 3 tests the system prompt against three real inputs and one edge case. If the edge case breaks the prompt, the participant asks Claude to fix it.

test_output.title: The Simplicity Test
test_output.description: Can you explain what your product does in one sentence? If no, the solution is still too complex.

test_output.title: The Flow Test
test_output.description: Can you describe the three-step user flow without hesitation? If no, the flow is not clear enough.

test_output.title: The System Prompt Test
test_output.description: Does your system prompt have all seven elements? If any element is missing, complete it before moving on.

test_output.title: The Edge Case Test
test_output.description: Has your system prompt been tested against at least one unexpected input? Does it handle it gracefully?

test_output.title: The HMV Test
test_output.description: Can you answer all three HMV questions without hesitation? Humanics — does the output serve the human? Mechanics — does the architecture reflect what AI can actually do? Valuenomics — does the output make the value immediately visible?

sessions.title: Build the HTML App
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a problem. You have a design. You have a system prompt. Now you build.

Today a solopreneur with a clear idea and the right prompts can have a working product with a live URL in ninety minutes. Not a mockup. Not a wireframe. Something real that a stranger can open on their phone right now.

One HTML file. No code knowledge required. No API. No backend. Everything runs in the browser. You will build it with Claude, customise it until it feels like yours, and deploy it to the internet before this session ends.

questions.title: What does the user see first?
questions.body: When your target user opens your app for the first time, what is the first impression? Not the design. The feeling.
questions.facilitator_note: Does that feeling match the problem the participant is solving?

questions.title: What is the app called and what does it say it does?
questions.body: Your app needs a name and a one-line description. The name should say something true. The description should tell the user exactly what they will get.
questions.facilitator_note: A name that says something versus a name that sounds impressive but tells the user nothing.

questions.title: How does the user interact with it?
questions.body: Walk through the user's physical interaction. What do they click? What do they type? What do they see changing on screen?
questions.facilitator_note: Every extra click is friction. Every unclear label is friction.

questions.title: What does the output look like?
questions.body: When the app produces its result, what does the user actually see? A wall of text? A structured plan? A numbered list? A scorecard?
questions.facilitator_note: Connect the output format to the user. A busy professional cannot read a wall of text.

questions.title: What makes it feel trustworthy?
questions.body: What specific element makes a sceptical user trust it enough to use the output?
questions.facilitator_note: The three trust signals for AI products are specificity, clarity, and honesty. Which is most important for this specific user?

questions.title: What happens after the output?
questions.body: When the user has their result, what do you want them to do next?
questions.facilitator_note: Every product has a natural next action. If the app does not guide the user toward it, most users close the tab and the value is lost.

techniques: The stranger test — open the app as if you have never seen it. Is it immediately obvious what to do?
The language test — read every label and button out loud. Does it sound like something a real person would say?
The completion test — would a real user complete the full journey without help?
The real input test — run the app with a real input, not a perfect test input. What happens?
The edge case test — run the app with an input it was not designed for. Does it fail gracefully?
The consistency test — run the same input twice. Do you get the same quality of output?
The first glance test — look at the output for three seconds and look away. What did you retain?
The worth test — if someone paid for this output, would they feel it was worth it?
The share test — would your target user screenshot this output and send it to a colleague?

prompts.title: Build the HTML App
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building my first AI-powered product. I need you to build me a complete single-page HTML app.

App name: [from Question 2]
One-line description: [from Question 2]
Target user: [from Session 1]
First impression feeling: [from Question 1]
What the user inputs: [from Session 2]
How the user interacts: [from Question 3]
What the output looks like: [from Question 4]
What makes it trustworthy: [from Question 5]
What happens after the output: [from Question 6]
System prompt for the logic: [from Session 2]

Build me a complete single-page HTML app. No external API calls. Everything runs in the browser. The design should feel professional and trustworthy.

After you build it, explain each section in plain language so I understand exactly what you built and why. I will read it carefully and ask you to make specific changes.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Stage 1 prompt. Participants paste, then read every section before moving on.

prompts.title: Refine and Personalise
prompts.content:
Read the HTML app you just built. I want to make the following specific changes.

Change 1: [specific change]
Change 2: [specific change]
Change 3: [specific change]

Make these changes and show me the updated file. Explain what you changed and why the change improves the app for my target user.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Stage 2 prompt. Specific changes only — no vague feedback.

build.title: Claude Builds (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant pastes Prompt 1 into Claude. Claude produces the complete HTML file with a plain-language explanation of every section. Participant reads every section carefully. Does not rush. Understands what each part does before moving on.

build.title: Participant Directs (20 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant identifies two or three specific things to change. Not vague feedback. Specific changes. They paste Prompt 2 with their specific changes. Claude updates the file. Participant reviews again.

build.title: Deploy on Netlify (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant goes to netlify.com. Creates a free account. Drags the HTML file into the browser. Netlify generates a live URL in two minutes. Opens the URL on their phone. Tests with one real input. Identifies one thing that needs improving. Fixes it. Redeploys.

test_output.title: The Live URL Test
test_output.description: Does your app have a live URL that works on a phone browser without any explanation from you?

test_output.title: The Stranger Test
test_output.description: Open the app as if you have never seen it. Is it immediately obvious what to do without reading any instructions?

test_output.title: The Real Input Test
test_output.description: Run the app with a real input your target user would actually provide. Does the output serve them genuinely?

test_output.title: The Trust Test
test_output.description: Does the output look like something a professional produced or like raw AI output? If it looks professional, pass.

test_output.title: The Worth Test
test_output.description: Would your target user feel the output was worth the time they spent providing the input?

sessions.title: Build the Website
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a product. It works. It lives at a real URL. But right now, nobody knows it exists.

A solopreneur without a website is a shopkeeper without a shop front. Your website is a conversation with a stranger who has never heard of you. A conversation that has to answer three questions in under thirty seconds.

What is this? Is this for me? What do I do next?

If your website answers those three questions clearly, the stranger clicks through to your app. If it does not, they leave. You have sixty minutes to build that conversation.

questions.title: What is the one thing your website must make a stranger feel?
questions.body: Not what it must say. What it must make them feel. In the first three seconds before they have read a single word.
questions.facilitator_note: Connect the feeling to the human portrait.

questions.title: What is the headline?
questions.body: One line. Maximum ten words. Product headlines describe what the product is. User headlines describe what changes for the user.
questions.facilitator_note: Which type is theirs? If a product headline, push them to rewrite it as a user headline.

questions.title: Why would your target user hesitate?
questions.body: Before clicking and trying your app, what is the one thing that makes them pause?
questions.facilitator_note: The three most common hesitations for AI products: whether it will work for their specific situation, whether they can trust the output, whether it will be complicated.

questions.title: What is the one action you want them to take?
questions.body: Not two actions. Not a menu of options. One. What is the single most important thing a visitor should do?
questions.facilitator_note: Almost always, the answer is try the app.

questions.title: Why should a stranger trust you?
questions.body: The more specific you are about who this is for, what it does, and what it does not do, the more a specific user trusts you.
questions.facilitator_note: Generic claims create generic trust which is no trust at all.

questions.title: How does it work in three steps?
questions.body: Describe how your product works in three steps. One sentence each. Simple enough that a person who has never used AI can understand it immediately.
questions.facilitator_note: Every piece of jargon is a barrier.

techniques: The pronoun test — count you versus I or we. You should appear far more than I or we.
The recognition test — would your target user read the headline and think this is written about me?
The conversation test — read the copy out loud as if speaking directly to your target user.
The accuracy test — does the website describe what the product actually does, not what you wish it did?
The clarity test — does the how it works section describe what the AI does in plain language?
The expectation test — after reading, does the visitor have an accurate expectation of what will happen?
The thirty-second test — after thirty seconds, can someone say what the product does and who it is for?
The specificity test — does the website name a specific outcome, not improve your productivity but save three hours?
The comparison test — does the website help the visitor understand why this is better than their current workaround?

prompts.title: Write the Website Copy
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building a marketing website for my product. Write the copy for six sections. Final words ready to paste. No explanations. Just the best version.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My value proposition: [from Session 1]
The feeling the website must create: [from Question 1]
My app URL: [Netlify URL]
My target user's primary hesitation: [from Question 3]

Section 1 — Headline. One line. Maximum ten words. A user headline, not a product headline.
Section 2 — Subheadline. Two sentences. Who this is for. What specifically changes.
Section 3 — How it works. Three steps. One sentence each. No jargon.
Section 4 — Trust statement. Directly addresses the primary hesitation. Honest. Specific.
Section 5 — Call to action. One button label that describes the action and hints at value.
Section 6 — Credibility line. One sentence. Why I am the right person to have built this.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Stage 1 prompt. Participant reads every section and checks against the questions.

prompts.title: Test and Refine
prompts.content:
Read the copy you just wrote as if you are my target user seeing this for the first time.

My target user is: [human portrait]

Ask yourself three questions.
Does the headline stop me and make me think this is for me?
Does the copy speak to me directly or could it apply to anyone?
After reading everything, do I know exactly what to do next and do I want to do it?

For any section where the answer is no, rewrite that section. Tell me what you changed and why.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Stage 2 prompt. Claude reads as the target user and identifies weaknesses.

build.title: Claude Writes the Copy (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant pastes Prompt 1 into Claude. Claude produces all six sections. Participant reads every section and checks against the questions. Identifies any section that feels generic or off-tone.

build.title: Claude Tests and Refines (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant pastes Prompt 2. Claude reads the copy as the target user and identifies weaknesses. Rewrites weak sections. Participant makes one final edit.

build.title: Build on Carrd (40 minutes)
build.instructions: Go to carrd.co. Open a blank canvas. Build the website section by section. Paste each piece of Claude's copy. Link the CTA button to the HTML app URL. Add one visual — a screenshot of the app running with a real output. Publish. Test on phone. Fix one thing. Republish.

test_output.title: The Thirty-Second Test
test_output.description: Show the website to someone in the room. After thirty seconds ask what does this do and who is it for. If they answer accurately, pass.

test_output.title: The Pronoun Test
test_output.description: Count the number of times the website says you versus I or we. You should appear significantly more. If not, rewrite toward the user.

test_output.title: The Hesitation Test
test_output.description: Does the website directly address the primary hesitation from Question 3? If no, add the trust statement.

test_output.title: The Link Test
test_output.description: Does the CTA button work and link to the correct HTML app URL? Open on a phone and tap the button.

test_output.title: The Expectation Test
test_output.description: After reading the website, does a visitor have an accurate expectation of what will happen when they try the app?

sessions.title: Build the Chatbot
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a product. You have a website. But not everyone who lands on your website is ready to try your product immediately. Some have questions. Some have doubts.

A chatbot is your always-on conversation partner. It handles the questions while you sleep. It addresses the doubts before they become reasons to leave. It guides the curious visitor toward becoming an actual user.

A good chatbot does not feel like a robot. It feels like a knowledgeable, helpful person who genuinely understands the product and cares about the visitor.

questions.title: What are the three questions your target user most wants answered?
questions.body: Not the questions you want to answer. The questions they actually have. The ones that sit between them reading your website and clicking the button.
questions.facilitator_note: User questions are almost always about trust, time, and specificity.

questions.title: What does your chatbot sound like?
questions.body: Your chatbot has a personality. Describe it in three words. Not the design. The personality.
questions.facilitator_note: Connect the personality to the human portrait. A tool for anxious founders needs a warm and reassuring chatbot. A tool for experienced consultants needs a sharp and direct one.

questions.title: What is the chatbot's opening line?
questions.body: The first thing a visitor reads when the chatbot appears. It must make the visitor feel that this is worth engaging with.
questions.facilitator_note: Does it acknowledge what the visitor is here for? Does it invite a natural response?

questions.title: What happens when the chatbot does not know the answer?
questions.body: Every chatbot reaches the edge of its knowledge.
questions.facilitator_note: Failure mode one is making something up — destroys trust instantly. Failure mode two is saying I do not know and stopping — leaves the visitor stranded. The right response acknowledges the limitation and offers a clear alternative.

questions.title: How does every conversation end?
questions.body: Regardless of what question a visitor asks, every conversation should end with one thing.
questions.facilitator_note: The job of the chatbot is to move the visitor one step closer to trying the app. What is that closing invitation for this specific product?

questions.title: What should the chatbot never say?
questions.body: Name two or three things that would make your target user immediately distrust or disengage.
questions.facilitator_note: The three most common chatbot trust-breakers are overclaiming, jargon, and deflection.

techniques: The natural language test — read every response out loud. Does it sound like a real person or like a FAQ page?
The empathy test — does the chatbot acknowledge what the visitor is feeling before answering their question?
The brevity test — every chatbot response should be shorter than it could be. Visitors scan, they do not read.
The flow test — can a visitor navigate from the opening line to the CTA without hitting a dead end?
The edge case test — what happens when a visitor says something completely unexpected?
The loop test — can the visitor ask a follow-up question after receiving an answer?
The conversion test — does every conversation end with a genuine invitation to try the product?
The qualification test — does the chatbot help visitors understand if the product is right for them?
The trust test — after a conversation, does the visitor trust the product more than before?

prompts.title: Design the Conversation Flow
prompts.content:
I am building a chatbot for my solopreneur product using Voiceflow. Design the complete conversation flow.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
Chatbot personality in three words: [from Question 2]
The three questions my user most wants answered: [from Question 1]
What the chatbot never says: [from Question 6]
How every conversation ends: [from Question 5]
What happens when the chatbot does not know: [from Question 4]

Design the complete conversation flow structured as follows.

Opening message — acknowledges what the visitor is here for, invites a specific response.
Three question-answer pairs — for each of the three user questions. Each answer honest, specific, brief.
One edge case — a question the chatbot was not designed to handle. Show the honest response.
Closing invitation — the natural, genuine invitation to try the product that ends every conversation.

Format as a clear conversation script I can build in Voiceflow.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Stage 1 prompt. Participant reads the full script out loud quietly — fastest way to identify unnatural language.

prompts.title: Test the Conversation
prompts.content:
Read the conversation flow you just designed. Now play the role of three different visitors.

Visitor 1 — a curious first-time visitor who has one specific question about whether this works for their situation.
Visitor 2 — a sceptical visitor who pushes back on one of the answers and asks a follow-up question the chatbot was not designed for.
Visitor 3 — a visitor who clicks through every option just to see what happens.

For each visitor, tell me honestly whether the chatbot served them well. Where does it break? Where does it lose them? What needs to change?
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Stage 1b prompt — runs immediately after Prompt 1 to find weak spots before Voiceflow build.

build.title: Claude Designs the Conversation (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant pastes Prompt 1 into Claude. Claude produces the complete conversation script. Participant reads the full script out loud quietly. Then pastes Prompt 2. Claude tests against three visitor types and identifies weaknesses.

build.title: Build in Voiceflow (35 minutes)
build.instructions: Go to voiceflow.com. Open a new project. Build the conversation block by block following Claude's script. Opening message block. Three question buttons. Three response blocks. Edge case response block. Closing invitation block. Publish. Get embed code. Paste embed code into Carrd website.

build.title: Test and Refine (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Open Carrd website on phone. The chatbot appears. Go through every path in the conversation as a real visitor would. Fix one thing in Voiceflow. Republish. Reload website. Test again.

test_output.title: The Live Test
test_output.description: Open the chatbot on the Carrd website. Go through every conversation path. Does every path reach the closing invitation without hitting a dead end?

test_output.title: The Natural Language Test
test_output.description: Read every chatbot response out loud. Does every response sound like a real, knowledgeable person?

test_output.title: The Edge Case Test
test_output.description: Type something completely unexpected into the chatbot. Does it respond gracefully with an honest response and clear alternative?

test_output.title: The Trust Test
test_output.description: Ask someone in the room to try the chatbot. After the conversation, do they trust the product more than before?

test_output.title: The Conversion Test
test_output.description: Does every conversation, regardless of which path, end with a genuine invitation to try the product?

sessions.title: Build the Audio — Overnight Assignment
sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at what you have built today. A working product with a live URL. A website that markets it. A chatbot that handles the questions. Three things that did not exist at 10am this morning.

Tonight you have one task. Thirty minutes. You do it at home, on your phone, or wherever you end up.

You are going to give your product a voice. Not a logo. Not a colour scheme. A literal voice. Sixty seconds of audio that captures who you are, what problem you solve, and why someone should try what you built.

You will write the script tonight using Claude. Tomorrow morning you will generate it using ElevenLabs and it will be live on your website before 11am.

questions.title: What is the one moment you want the listener to feel?
questions.body: Not what you want them to know. What you want them to feel. At the end of sixty seconds, what is the one emotion you want them to carry?
questions.facilitator_note: Curiosity. Recognition. Confidence. Which emotion connected to which moment in the target user's life?

questions.title: Whose voice is this?
questions.body: Founder voice is personal, credible, builds connection. Works when the founder's story is relevant to the user's problem. Product voice is direct, efficient, focused on the user. Works when the problem is universally felt.
questions.facilitator_note: Which serves this specific target user better?

questions.title: What is the problem in one sentence — spoken out loud?
questions.body: Say the problem your product solves out loud. Right now. Without reading anything.
questions.facilitator_note: The spoken version is almost always better than the written version. Have them record it. That is the audio opening.

questions.title: What does your product do — explained to a friend?
questions.body: Explain your product to the person sitting next to you as if you are telling a friend about something useful you discovered. Casual. No jargon. No pitch.
questions.facilitator_note: That casual explanation is the middle section of the audio.

questions.title: What is the one thing you want the listener to do after sixty seconds?
questions.body: Not two things. One. An invitation makes someone want to do it.
questions.facilitator_note: A call to action tells. An invitation makes them want.

techniques: The spoken test — read the script out loud before generating. Every sentence that feels unnatural to say will sound unnatural when generated.
The breath test — are there natural pause points? Audio without breathing space is exhausting to listen to.
The specificity test — does the audio describe the specific problem and the specific user?
The length test — time the script. Read at a natural pace. It should land between fifty-five and sixty-five seconds.
The clarity test — are there any words difficult to hear clearly when spoken? Replace with clearer alternatives.
The ElevenLabs test — after generating, listen to the first five seconds. If it does not sound natural, try a different voice.
The platform test — where will this audio be shared beyond the website?
The memorability test — after listening once, can the listener remember what the product does and where to find it?
The shareability test — would a listener who found this genuinely useful forward it to someone else?

prompts.title: The Overnight Prompt
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building a sixty-second audio piece for my brand. This is the voice of my product.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My value proposition: [from Session 1]
Whose voice this is: [founder or product — from Question 2]
The problem in my own words: [what they said out loud in Q3]
How I explained it to a friend: [the casual explanation from Q4]
The one thing I want the listener to do: [from Question 5]
The feeling I want them to carry: [from Question 1]

Write me a sixty-second audio script structured in three beats.

Beat 1 — fifteen seconds. The problem. In the words my target user would use. Specific. Felt. Not generic.
Beat 2 — thirty seconds. The solution. Conversational. Clear. No jargon.
Beat 3 — fifteen seconds. The invitation. Natural. Not a call to action.

Write for the ear. Short sentences. Conversational rhythm. Natural pause points marked with [pause].

After the script, tell me the total word count and approximate speaking time at a natural pace.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Send this prompt with participants for the overnight task. Tomorrow morning Session 6B picks up with ElevenLabs generation.

build.title: Overnight — Self-Directed
build.instructions: Participant opens Claude on their phone or laptop tonight. Pastes the prompt. Reads the script Claude produces. Reads it out loud immediately. Identifies any sentence that feels unnatural to say. Asks Claude to rewrite that sentence. Reads again. Saves the final script. Brings it to Day 2.

test_output.title: The Overnight Readiness Test
test_output.description: The script is ready when it can be read out loud naturally from start to finish without any sentence feeling forced. Time it. If it falls outside fifty-five to sixty-five seconds, trim or expand.

sessions.title: Build the Audio — Morning Execution
sessions.set_the_stage:
Last night you wrote something real. A script that captures your problem, your product, and your invitation in sixty seconds. This morning you give it a voice.

ElevenLabs is one of the most remarkable tools in the no-code AI landscape. You paste in text. You choose a voice. You press generate. In thirty seconds you have professional-quality audio that sounds like it was recorded in a studio.

Ten minutes to generate. Ten minutes to embed. Ten minutes to test. By 11am your website has a voice.

questions.title: What does your brand sound like?
questions.body: Name a public figure, a podcaster, a presenter, or a type of voice that matches how you want your brand to sound.
questions.facilitator_note: This primes their ear before opening ElevenLabs.

questions.title: Where on your website does the audio live?
questions.body: One specific place.
questions.facilitator_note: The most effective placements are below the headline (after the visitor has read what the product is) or below the how-it-works section (as a human confirmation of what they just learned).

build.title: Generate on ElevenLabs (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Go to elevenlabs.io. Create a free account or log in. Go to Text to Speech. Paste the script. Browse the voice library. Select a voice matching the feeling from Question 1. Generate. Listen to the full sixty seconds. Identify one moment that sounds unnatural. Edit that moment. Regenerate. Download the MP3 file.

build.title: Embed on Website (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Go to Carrd website. Open the editor. Add an audio element at the placement identified in Question 2. Upload the MP3. Set it to play on click, not autoplay. Add a one-line label above it. Something like: Hear why I built this. Sixty seconds. Publish the updated website.

build.title: Test (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Open the website on phone. Navigate to the audio element. Press play. Listen as the target user would listen. Identify one moment where attention drifts. Edit that moment. Regenerate. Re-embed. Test again.

test_output.title: The Natural Speech Test
test_output.description: Listen to the entire sixty seconds without reading the script. Does every sentence sound like something a real person would genuinely say?

test_output.title: The Timing Test
test_output.description: Does the audio land between fifty-five and sixty-five seconds at a natural listening pace?

test_output.title: The Live Embed Test
test_output.description: Open the website on a phone. Navigate to the audio element. Does it play when clicked?

test_output.title: The Retention Test
test_output.description: After listening once, can you name the problem, the solution, and the next step without looking at the script?

test_output.title: The Stranger Test
test_output.description: Play the audio to someone in the room who has not heard it before. After listening, ask what does this product do? If they answer accurately, pass.

sessions.title: Build the Video
sessions.break_note: Validation conversations happen during this lunch. Every participant leaves the room with their five validation questions ready, calls or messages one real potential user, and takes verbatim notes during the conversation. Synthesis happens in Session 9.
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a product. A website. A chatbot. An audio piece. Now you need video.

Text tells someone what you built. Audio lets them hear your conviction. Video shows them the product working in real time. No description. No imagination required. They see it happen.

A ninety-second screen recording of your product doing its job, with you narrating over it, is the most powerful piece of content you will produce today. It travels. People share videos.

Production quality does not matter. What matters is authenticity. A polished corporate video creates distance. A real person showing a real product doing something genuinely useful creates connection.

questions.title: What is the most impressive moment in your product?
questions.body: If you could show a sceptical person one thing, what would it be?
questions.facilitator_note: The most impressive moment is almost never the homepage. It is the output. The moment the result appears. That moment is the centrepiece of the video.

questions.title: What does the viewer need to believe before they see that moment?
questions.body: Before the impressive moment lands, what does the viewer need to understand or feel?
questions.facilitator_note: The setup is showing the viewer that the problem is real, the current solution is inadequate, and what they are about to see addresses both. A magic trick without setup is just a trick. With setup, it is a revelation.

questions.title: What do you say while the screen is showing?
questions.body: Not a script yet. Just the feeling. Excited? Calm and confident? Quietly proud? Conversational?
questions.facilitator_note: The tone of narration should match what the target user needs to feel to trust what they are seeing.

questions.title: What is the one sentence that describes the problem?
questions.body: Say it out loud. Right now. The problem your product solves in one sentence spoken the way you would say it to a friend.
questions.facilitator_note: This sentence opens the video. It must be said, not read. The viewer hears immediately whether the narrator genuinely cares about the problem.

questions.title: Where does this video live after you record it?
questions.body: Not just the website. Where else? LinkedIn? WhatsApp? An email to a potential user?
questions.facilitator_note: A video posted to LinkedIn and shared nowhere else reaches a fraction of its potential audience. Name the specific places before recording.

questions.title: What do you want the viewer to do in the thirty seconds after the video ends?
questions.body: Not eventually. In the thirty seconds immediately after, while the impression is still fresh, what one action do you want them to take?
questions.facilitator_note: The closing of the video must name that action explicitly and make it feel natural.

techniques: The authenticity test — watch the video back with the sound off. Does the narrator look like someone who genuinely cares?
The pacing test — is the narration rushing? Slow down. Pause after the impressive moment. Let the viewer absorb it.
The specificity test — does the video show a real input from a real user, even if that user is the participant themselves?
The clarity test — is the text on screen large enough to read on a phone? Zoom the browser to 125 percent before recording.
The reliability test — run through the demo once before recording. Make sure the product works exactly as expected.
The distraction test — is the screen clean? Notifications off? Browser tabs minimised?
The show not tell test — does the video spend more time showing the product working than talking about it?
The outcome test — does the video show the output in enough detail that the viewer can evaluate its quality?
The action test — after watching, is the next step obvious and accessible?

prompts.title: Write the Narration Script
prompts.content:
I am recording a ninety-second screen recording demo of my product using Loom. Write me the narration script.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
The problem in one sentence spoken: [from Question 4]
The most impressive moment in my product: [from Question 1]
What the viewer needs to believe before that moment: [from Q2]
Narration tone: [from Question 3]
What I want the viewer to do immediately after watching: [from Q6]
Where the link to my product lives: [website URL]

Write the narration script in three beats.

Beat 1 — thirty seconds. The problem. Spoken in the words my target user would use. Sets up the impressive moment.
Beat 2 — forty-five seconds. The demonstration. What I am showing on screen. Written so it sounds natural when spoken over a live screen recording.
Beat 3 — fifteen seconds. The invitation. Natural. Specific. Names the one action and where to take it.

Mark each beat clearly. Mark any moments where I should pause and let the screen speak for itself.

After the script, give me five specific things to show on screen during Beat 2 in the exact order to show them.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Read the script out loud twice before recording. Make any edits that feel unnatural to speak.

build.title: Prepare (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Read the script out loud twice. Make any edits that feel unnatural to speak. Do not memorise. Keep the script visible while recording. Clean the browser screen. Close unnecessary tabs. Zoom to 125 percent. Turn off notifications. Open the product HTML app. Prepare a real input. Open Loom. Set to record screen and camera simultaneously.

build.title: Record (20 minutes)
build.instructions: One take. Not perfect. Real. Speak from the script but do not read word for word. Pause after the impressive moment. Let the output sit on screen for three full seconds in silence. That silence is more powerful than anything the narrator could say. End with the website URL spoken out loud.

build.title: Post and Embed (30 minutes)
build.instructions: Loom generates a shareable link immediately after recording. Post to LinkedIn immediately. No editing. No production. The post caption is one paragraph: the problem in two sentences and the invitation in one. Take the embed code from Loom and embed the video on the Carrd website below the how it works section. Open the website on phone. Confirm the video works.

test_output.title: The Live Post Test
test_output.description: Is the video posted on LinkedIn with a caption? Open LinkedIn and confirm the post is live and the video plays.

test_output.title: The Embed Test
test_output.description: Open the Carrd website on a phone. Navigate to the video. Does it play without needing to leave the website?

test_output.title: The Impressive Moment Test
test_output.description: Watch the video back. Does the impressive moment appear clearly on screen? Does the narrator pause long enough for the viewer to absorb it?

test_output.title: The Stranger Test
test_output.description: Show the video to someone in the room who has not seen your product. After watching, ask what does this product do and what would you do next? If they answer both accurately, pass.

test_output.title: The Action Test
test_output.description: After the video ends, is the next step obvious and accessible to someone watching on a phone?

sessions.title: Build the Agent
sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at everything you have built. A product that works. A website that markets it. A chatbot that handles questions. An audio piece that gives it a voice. A video that shows it in action.

All of it is visible. All of it requires someone to find it, open it, and engage with it. Now you build the invisible layer. The thing that runs while you sleep. While you are in meetings. While you are building the next version.

That is what an agent is. Not an AI chatbot. Not a product feature. An automated workflow that takes one trigger and produces one action, reliably, repeatedly, without you.

One trigger. One action. Real and live before this session ends.

questions.title: What is the one thing you would most want to happen automatically?
questions.body: Not a list. One thing. The thing that if it happened without you every time would make the biggest difference to how your business runs.
questions.facilitator_note: Two categories: user-facing automations that improve the experience for the person using the product, and solopreneur-facing automations that make your life easier.

questions.title: What triggers it?
questions.body: Something has to start the automation.
questions.facilitator_note: The most reliable triggers for solopreneur builds: a form submission, a scheduled time, a new row in a spreadsheet, a new email in an inbox, or a webhook.

questions.title: What is the one action that follows?
questions.body: When the trigger fires, what happens? One action. Not a sequence of five steps.
questions.facilitator_note: The most common mistake is making it too complex. A trigger that fires and sends one email is powerful. One trigger and one action. That is the brief.

questions.title: Who benefits from this automation and how?
questions.body: When this agent runs, who notices? Who benefits? What changes for them?
questions.facilitator_note: The best automations are invisible to the user in the right way. They do not think it was automated. They think that was fast. That was helpful. That felt personal.

questions.title: How do you know it is working?
questions.body: After you build this and turn it on, how do you know it fired?
questions.facilitator_note: The simplest confirmation is a notification to yourself. An email. A WhatsApp message. You cannot trust an agent you cannot verify.

techniques: The personal test — does the automated message feel written for this specific person or like a mass email with a name inserted?
The timing test — does the agent fire at the right moment in the user's journey?
The tone test — does the automated message sound like the same person who wrote the website copy and narrated the video?
The reliability test — test the trigger three times before turning the agent on for real users.
The failure test — what happens when the trigger fires with incomplete or unexpected data?
The permission test — does the agent have the correct permissions to perform its action?
The time test — how much time does this automation save per week? If less than thirty minutes, it is a nice-to-have.
The compounding test — does this automation become more valuable as the business grows?
The leverage test — does this automation free the solopreneur to do something more valuable with their time?

prompts.title: Design the Agent
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building my first automated agent using Make.com. Help me design a simple, reliable one-trigger one-action automation.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My website URL: [from Session 4]
My HTML app URL: [from Session 3]

The automation I want to build: [from Question 1]
The trigger: [from Question 2]
The action: [from Question 3]
Who benefits and how: [from Question 4]
How I know it is working: [from Question 5]

Do three things.

First, tell me honestly whether this is achievable in Make.com on a free account in thirty minutes. If not, suggest a simpler version.

Second, give me a step by step description of how to build this in Make.com. What modules to use. What to connect. What to configure. Simple enough for someone who has never used Make.com.

Third, write the content of the automated action. If it is an email, write the subject line and body. Write it so it sounds personal and human. Not automated. Not templated.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Two checks before they start building: Is this achievable on Make.com free tier in thirty minutes? Is the automated message genuinely personal?

build.title: Claude Designs the Automation (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Participant pastes the prompt into Claude. Reads the response carefully. Two things to check immediately. Is this achievable on Make.com free tier in thirty minutes? Is the automated message genuinely personal?

build.title: Build in Make.com (25 minutes)
build.instructions: Go to make.com. Create a free account or log in. Create a new scenario. Follow Claude's step-by-step instructions. One module at a time. The trigger module configured. The action module connected. Test using Make.com's built-in test function before going live. Run the test three times. Confirm the action fires correctly each time. Turn the scenario on.

build.title: Confirm and Verify (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Trigger the automation manually one final time. Confirm the action completed. Confirm the confirmation notification reached you. Take a screenshot of the Make.com scenario showing it is active.

test_output.title: The Live Test
test_output.description: Trigger the automation manually right now. Does the action complete within the expected timeframe?

test_output.title: The Personal Test
test_output.description: Read the automated message or action output. Does it feel written for a specific person or like mass communication? If personal, pass.

test_output.title: The Reliability Test
test_output.description: Trigger the automation three times in a row. Does it fire correctly all three times?

test_output.title: The Verification Test
test_output.description: Do you receive confirmation when the agent fires? Can you tell from your own inbox or dashboard that the automation ran?

test_output.title: The Value Test
test_output.description: What specifically changes for the user or for you every time this agent fires? Can you name it in one sentence? If yes, the automation creates real value.

sessions.title: Validation
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have built six things. Every single one of them was built on assumptions. You assumed you understood the problem. You assumed your user would find the interface clear. You assumed the output was valuable enough to act on.

Some of those assumptions are right. Some are wrong. And right now you do not know which are which.

The only way to find out is to put what you built in front of a real person who has the problem. Not a friend who wants to support you. Not a colleague who is being polite. Someone who genuinely has the problem and has no reason to be kind.

During lunch you had sixty minutes for one conversation with one real person. Not a presentation. A conversation. You showed them what you built. You asked five specific questions. You listened without defending. You wrote down exactly what they said in their words, not yours.

Now we synthesise.

questions.title: Who are you calling?
questions.body: Name one specific person you can reach in the next sixty minutes who genuinely has the problem. Not a friend. Not a family member. Someone who has the problem and has no personal reason to be kind.
questions.facilitator_note: Asked at the end of Session 7 before lunch. If they cannot name a real person, ask the cohort for help.

questions.title: How do you open the conversation?
questions.body: The problem-curiosity approach works better than the product-pitch approach. Instead of I have built something and would love your feedback, try: I have been working on something related to a problem I think you deal with. Before I show you what I built, can I ask you about your experience with it?
questions.facilitator_note: This puts the person in reflector mode rather than evaluator mode.

questions.title: What are you listening for?
questions.body: Three most valuable signals. Language signals — the specific words they use to describe the problem. Hesitation signals — the moment they pause before answering. Willingness signals — whether they ask follow-up questions or where to find the product.
questions.facilitator_note: These signals matter more than the content of what they say.

questions.title: What will you do when they say something that challenges your assumptions?
questions.body: Not if. When. You do not defend. You do not explain. You do not say yes but what I meant was. You write it down exactly as they said it. You ask can you tell me more about that. You listen.
questions.facilitator_note: The instinct to defend is the enemy of validation.

questions.title: What is the one question you are most afraid to ask?
questions.body: The question that would be most valuable to have answered but that you have been avoiding. Almost always the answer is: would you pay for this?
questions.facilitator_note: Ask the question you are most afraid to ask. That is where the most important information lives.

techniques: Listen to the language. Write down their exact words. These are your next iteration brief.
Watch for the emotional response. When they see the output for the first time, what do they do? Do they lean in?
Ask about the experience not the product. Walk me through what happened when you used it produces more than what do you think of this?
Test before calling. Run the product with a real input before the conversation starts.
Show first, explain second. Do not explain what the product does before showing it.
Do not apologise for limitations. Note them, thank them, and move on.
Ask about the current cost. How do they currently handle this problem? How long does it take? What does it cost?
Ask about the output value. After they see the output, what would they do with it?
Ask the payment question directly. Would you pay for access to this? What would need to be different?

prompts.title: Prepare the Five Validation Questions
prompts.content:
I am about to have a validation conversation with a real potential user. Give me five specific questions to ask in the right order.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My primary assumption about the problem: [the core belief the product is built on]
The question I am most afraid to ask: [from Question 5]
What I most need to learn: [one specific thing]

Give me five questions ordered for a natural conversation flow.

Question 1 — opens on the problem before showing the product.
Question 2 — explores the current workaround and its cost.
Question 3 — follows after showing the product. Asks about experience, not rating.
Question 4 — explores the specific value of the output.
Question 5 — the question I am most afraid to ask, worded to invite honest reflection.

After each question, give me one follow-up probe that goes deeper when the person gives a surface-level answer.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Run this prompt before lunch. Participants prepare their five questions, then call or message during lunch.

prompts.title: Synthesise What You Heard
prompts.content:
I just had a validation conversation. Here is what I heard.

What they said about the problem: [verbatim notes]
What they said about the current workaround: [verbatim notes]
Their reaction when they saw the product: [what they did and said]
What they said about the output: [verbatim notes]
Their answer to the payment question: [verbatim notes]
The one thing they said that I did not expect: [verbatim notes]

Analyse what I heard through three lenses.

Humanics — what did this conversation reveal about my target user that I did not know before?
Mechanics — what did this reveal about how the product actually works versus how I assumed it worked?
Valuenomics — what did this reveal about the real value of the product?

End with three specific changes I should make based on what I heard.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Run this prompt at synthesis time (13:45). Each participant pastes their verbatim notes. Reads the synthesis before the room shares.

build.title: The Conversation (during lunch)
build.instructions: Participant identifies their validation target before leaving the room. Has the five questions ready on their phone. During lunch, makes the call or sends the WhatsApp message. Takes verbatim notes. Writes down exact words. After the conversation, pastes Prompt 2 into Claude. Reads the synthesis before returning to the room.

build.title: The Synthesis (13:45 to 14:00)
build.instructions:
After the agent session, the room comes together for fifteen minutes. Facilitator asks three questions simultaneously. Everyone writes their answer before sharing.

What is the most surprising thing you heard?
What did your validation target say that you wrote down in their exact words?
What are you changing because of this conversation?

Three people share each answer. Facilitator maps patterns across the cohort. What multiple participants heard about trust. About output quality. About the gap between assumed and experienced value. These patterns are the most important learnings of the entire program.

test_output.title: The Conversation Test
test_output.description: Did you have a real conversation with a real person who genuinely has the problem? Not a friend. Someone who has the problem and no personal reason to be kind.

test_output.title: The Language Test
test_output.description: Can you name three specific words or phrases your validation target used to describe the problem that were different from the words you have been using? These words belong in your website copy, your chatbot, and your video.

test_output.title: The Surprise Test
test_output.description: Can you name one specific thing your validation target said that genuinely surprised you? If no, the conversation was too comfortable.

test_output.title: The Payment Test
test_output.description: Did you ask whether they would pay for the product? What did they say? If you asked and got a clear answer, pass. If you did not ask, schedule the next conversation and ask it then.

test_output.title: The Change Test
test_output.description: Can you name three specific changes you will make based on what you heard? If yes, the validation produced actionable intelligence.

sessions.title: Internal Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at this room. It looks different from this morning. That is intentional.

What is about to happen is different from everything that came before it. For the last two days you have been building. Heads down. Focused on the product. Now you shift. From builder to founder.

A founder does not just build things. A founder can stand in front of people who do not know them, who have not been on this journey, who have no reason to be impressed, and make them understand in four minutes why what they built matters.

That is what Demo Day is. Not a show and tell. Not a progress update. A demonstration to real human beings that something real exists and that it creates real value.

The cohort in this room is your first real audience. They have been on the same journey as you. And precisely because they understand, they will not let you get away with vague claims and impressive-sounding words. You have fifteen minutes to prepare. Then you present.

questions.title: What is the problem in one sentence — spoken as if to a stranger?
questions.body: Not the version you have been saying in this room all day. The version a complete stranger would understand in ten seconds. Write it down. Say it out loud to yourself.
questions.facilitator_note: Circulate and identify any problem statement that is too technical, too long, or still sounds like a solution.

questions.title: What is the sequence of your six modalities?
questions.body: You have six things to show. The recommended sequence moves from product to ecosystem. HTML app first. Website second. Chatbot embedded on the website third. Audio fourth. Video fifth. Agent sixth.
questions.facilitator_note: By the end, the cohort sees not just a product but a complete solopreneur business.

questions.title: What is your impressive moment?
questions.body: The single moment during the four minutes when you want the room to feel that is genuinely good.
questions.facilitator_note: Do not say and here is the impressive part. Just show it. Pause. Let the room react. The pause is the framing. Trust it.

questions.title: What do you say when something does not work?
questions.body: Something will not work perfectly. Two acceptable responses. Narrate it: in the final version this will produce a specific output, what you are seeing is a limitation I identified during validation. Or pivot: move to the next modality without dwelling, come back to it in what is next.
questions.facilitator_note: What is not acceptable is apologising repeatedly or abandoning the demo structure.

questions.title: What is the one specific next action you commit to in the Build Month?
questions.body: Not three things. Not a plan. One thing.
questions.facilitator_note: The specificity of this commitment is one of the things the cohort will evaluate. A vague commitment signals unclear thinking. A specific commitment signals genuine learning.

techniques: Speak to the problem before showing the product. The audience needs to feel the problem before evaluating the solution.
Make eye contact during the impressive moment. Not at the screen. At the audience.
Tell the truth about what is not working. Honesty about limitations makes everything else more credible.
Use a real input. Not a perfect test case. A real input a real user would actually provide.
Test before presenting. Every modality. Every link. Every embed. Every trigger.
Have a phone backup. If the laptop fails, the website, chatbot, and video all exist on the phone.
Let the output speak. After the impressive moment, do not immediately start talking. Let the audience read.
Name the specific person this is for. Not professionals. The specific person from the human portrait.
Connect the agent to the business. Show that the presenter is building a business, not just a product.

prompts.title: Structure the Demo
prompts.content:
I am about to present at Demo Day. I have four minutes. I have six modalities to show. Help me structure the presentation.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My problem statement for the demo: [from Question 1]
My impressive moment: [from Question 3]
What did not work perfectly today: [from Question 4]
My one specific next action: [from Question 5]

Give me the exact structure for my four-minute demo.

Opening — thirty seconds. The problem statement. Spoken directly to the audience. No product yet.
Modality sequence — three minutes. Six modalities in order. For each one, one sentence of narration before showing it. What to say during the impressive moment. What to say if something does not work.
Closing — thirty seconds. What I learned from validation. My one specific next action. Said as a commitment not a plan.

Make the opening and closing memorable. The audience will remember the first thing they hear and the last thing they hear.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Fifteen minutes prep. Each participant runs the prompt and uses Claude's structure to rehearse once before presenting.

build.title: The Problem (30 seconds)
build.instructions: Spoken directly to the audience. Not read from notes. One sentence on the problem. One sentence on who has it. One number — what it costs them. Then silence. Two seconds. Let the problem land before showing the solution.

build.title: The Live Product (3 minutes)
build.instructions: Open the HTML app. Enter a real input. Show the output. Pause on the impressive moment. Move to the website. Show the headline. Click through to the app from the CTA button. Show the chatbot. Type one real question. Show the response. Play the audio for fifteen seconds. Play the first fifteen seconds of the video from LinkedIn. Show the Make.com scenario. Trigger it. Show the confirmation.

build.title: What Is Next (30 seconds)
build.instructions: One thing learned from validation. Spoken in the validation target's exact words. One specific change committed to for the Build Month. Named precisely. One sentence close. Not a thank you. A statement of intent.

build.title: After Each Demo — The Cohort Responds
build.instructions: Sixty seconds. Every participant writes one word or one short phrase. Simultaneously. Then shares. Not feedback. An impression. Compelling. Needed. Confused. Want this. The facilitator reads the pattern across responses. Names it directly. What the room agreed on. What divided the room. What surprised the room.

test_output.title: The Live Product Test
test_output.description: Did every modality work during the demo? HTML app produced a real output. Website CTA link worked. Chatbot responded. Audio played. Video played. Agent fired. If all six worked, pass.

test_output.title: The Stranger Test
test_output.description: Could someone who knew nothing about your product before the demo describe what it does, who it is for, and what makes it valuable using only what they saw and heard?

test_output.title: The Impressive Moment Test
test_output.description: Did you pause after the impressive moment? Did the room react? If you rushed through it without pausing, the next demo should correct this.

test_output.title: The Honesty Test
test_output.description: Did you acknowledge at least one thing that is not yet perfect? Did you connect it to what you learned from validation?

test_output.title: The Commitment Test
test_output.description: Did you name one specific next action? Was it specific enough that the cohort could hold you accountable in four weeks? Vague: I will improve the product. Specific: I will rewrite the output section using the three phrases my validation target used.

sessions.title: Close and Four-Week Brief
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop for a moment. Look at what you have in front of you right now. Forty-eight hours ago this did not exist. None of it. Not the product. Not the website. Not the chatbot. Not the audio. Not the video. Not the agent.

You came in with an idea on a card. You leave with six live things in the world. Things a stranger can find, open, hear, watch, and use without you being present to explain them.

But here is the thing most programs get wrong at this moment. They call it a celebration. They hand out certificates. They send people home with the feeling that something is complete. Nothing is complete.

What happened this weekend is the beginning, not the end. The real work starts now. The real signal about whether this idea has legs starts now. The next four weeks are harder than this weekend. This weekend you had structure. You had a facilitator. You had sessions and prompts and checkpoints. The next four weeks you have none of that.

That is what makes the Build Month the real test. Not whether you could build something in two days. Whether you can take what you built, put it in front of real people, learn from what they tell you, change something substantial, and come back to Demo Day with something that is genuinely better.

questions.title: What is the one thing you learned this weekend that you did not expect to learn?
questions.body: Not what you expected to learn. Not what was on the agenda. The one thing that surprised you.
questions.facilitator_note: Every participant answers. Not three people. Everyone. In sequence around the room. One sentence each. The facilitator listens for patterns. The surprises that appear across multiple participants are the most important learnings from the program.

questions.title: What is the gap between what you built and what your validation target needed?
questions.body: Be specific. Not they wanted more features. What specifically was different between what you built and what they actually needed?
questions.facilitator_note: The more specific the gap, the more actionable the Build Month.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will change first?
questions.body: Of everything you could improve, what is the one change that would make the biggest difference to your target user in the next week?
questions.facilitator_note: Apply the HMV lens to each answer. Is this a Humanics change, a Mechanics change, or a Valuenomics change?

questions.title: Who are the next three real people you will show this to?
questions.body: Not after you improve it. Now. This week. Before anything changes. Three specific people who have the problem and have no reason to be kind.
questions.facilitator_note: Anyone who cannot name three specific people before leaving this room is at risk of spending Week 1 improving in isolation rather than testing against reality.

questions.title: What would Demo Day success look like for you specifically?
questions.body: Not the program's definition of success. Yours. What would you need to have built, learned, or proven in four weeks for you personally to feel this was worth the time and commitment?
questions.facilitator_note: Every participant answers in writing first. Then shared with one other person in the room.

techniques: One real user conversation per week. Minimum. Not a survey. Not an email. A conversation, synchronous, live, where you can hear the hesitations.
Every change made to the product must be traceable to something a real person said.
If you find yourself improving the product based on your own ideas for more than two days without checking against a real user, stop. Book a conversation before continuing.
The most common failure mode in the Build Month is spending all four weeks rebuilding the product from scratch instead of iterating on what exists.
The product you built this weekend is good enough to test. It is not good enough to be the final version. Those are different things.

Every time you feel the urge to rebuild from scratch, ask one question. Am I rebuilding because the product is fundamentally wrong or because I am uncomfortable showing something imperfect to real people? Almost always it is the second reason.
Week 1 — ask one person if they would pay. Get a yes or a no and understand why.
Week 2 — ask three more. Look for a pattern.

Week 3 — if the pattern is yes, explore what paying would look like. If the pattern is no, ask what would need to change. Week 4 — come to Demo Day with a clear, honest answer to whether this creates value someone would pay for.

prompts.title: Build the Four-Week Plan
prompts.content:
I have just completed a two-day Just Build workshop. I have built six modalities of a solopreneur product. I now have four weeks to iterate and prepare for Demo Day.

My product: [one sentence solution]
What I learned from validation: [the key insight from Session 9]
The gap between what I built and what my user needed: [from Question 2]
The first change I will make: [from Question 3]
The three people I will show it to this week: [from Question 4]
What Demo Day success looks like for me: [from Question 5]

Build me a four-week plan. Structured and specific. One primary goal per week. Three specific actions per week. One checkpoint question that tells me whether I am on track.

Week 1 — Refine. Based on validation. One specific change. Three specific conversations.
Week 2 — Validate deeper. More real users. Clearer pattern. First willingness to pay conversation.
Week 3 — Iterate substantially. One meaningful change to the product based on what Weeks 1 and 2 revealed.
Week 4 — Prepare and launch. Demo Day ready. Product polished enough to show confidently.

For each week, give me the one question I should be able to answer by Friday that tells me the week was not wasted.
prompts.facilitator_instruction: Each participant runs the prompt. Reads the plan Claude produces. Makes two specific edits where Claude's generic plan needs adapting to their specific idea. Saves the plan.

build.title: The Four-Week Plan (20 minutes)
build.instructions: Every participant runs the prompt. Reads the plan Claude produces. Makes two specific edits where Claude's generic plan needs to be adapted to their specific idea. Saves the plan. This document is the operating brief for the next four weeks.

build.title: The Public Commitments (20 minutes)
build.instructions:
Every participant stands. States three things to the room.

1. The one specific change they commit to making in Week 1. Named precisely.
2. The three specific people they will show the product to before next Friday. Named by first name or relationship.
3. What Demo Day success looks like for them specifically. Said as a personal commitment, not a program requirement.

The cohort witnesses each commitment. The WhatsApp group captures them. In four weeks on Demo Day, every participant is held to what they said in this room. Vague commitments are gently pushed back on in real time.

build.title: The Four-Week Structure
build.instructions:
Weekly Group Session — every week, same day, same time, 90 minutes. Ten min: what did you change and why? Twenty min: what did you learn from real user conversations? Twenty min: what surprised you about how the product is behaving in the world? Twenty min: what are you changing next week? Twenty min: open questions to the cohort.

Office Hours — twice per week, 30 minutes each, bookable. For participants stuck on something specific. Come with the problem in one sentence. Leave with a clear next step.

WhatsApp Group — always on. Three uses only: sharing what a real user said (verbatim quotes), asking for help when stuck for more than two hours, celebrating when something works.

build.title: The Certificate Moment
build.instructions:
Read aloud:

You will notice you are not receiving a certificate today. That is deliberate. The OC Just Build Certificate marks one thing — completion of something real. Not attendance at a workshop. Not building something that works in a controlled environment. Completion of the full journey from idea to working product to real users responding to it.

That journey is not complete yet. Four weeks from now at Demo Day, when you stand in front of a real audience and demonstrate a product that is genuinely better than what you showed today, and when you can speak honestly about what you learned from real users and what you changed because of it, that is when the certificate means something. It will mean something because you earned it through the hardest part. Not the building. The iterating. The validating. The showing up week after week.

See you at Demo Day.

build.title: The Alumni Launch
build.instructions:
The WhatsApp group name changes from Just Build Cohort to OC Alumni, Cohort number.

Read aloud:

This group is yours. Not just for four weeks. Permanently. The people in this room are the people who understand what you went through this weekend. Use it. Help each other. Share what you learn. Introduce each other to real users. Challenge each other when the weekly commitments start to drift. The cohort that supports each other through the Build Month produces better Demo Days than the cohort that disappears until Week 4.

test_output.title: The Plan Test
test_output.description: Do you have a specific four-week plan with one primary goal per week, three specific actions per week, and one checkpoint question per week?

test_output.title: The Commitment Test
test_output.description: Did you state three specific public commitments to the room? Are they specific enough that the cohort could evaluate whether you kept them at Demo Day?

test_output.title: The People Test
test_output.description: Can you name three specific real people you will show the product to before next Friday? If you cannot name them, name them now before leaving the room.

test_output.title: The Success Test
test_output.description: Can you complete this sentence without hesitation? Demo Day success for me looks like — [specific, personal, measurable].

test_output.title: The Group Test
test_output.description: Are you in the WhatsApp alumni group? Have you saved the weekly session date and time in your calendar? Do you know how to book office hours?
